evidence_quotes.quote: Limitations on predatory monetization, especially where content is gated behind real-money transactions with built-in FOMO mechanics Loot boxes, gacha rolls, and battle passes should be subject to fairness requirements, especially when combined with time limits, unclear odds, or psychological manipulation.
the long-term accessibility and preservation of video games, especially those dependent on online services and monetized ecosystems.

paragraphs.text: To whom it may concern, As a lifelong gamer and consumer, I welcome the initiative behind the Digital Fairness Act. However, I would like to highlight a vital area that deserves greater attention within the scope of digital fairness: the long-term accessibility and preservation of video games, especially those dependent on online services and monetized ecosystems. In todays market, many games are no longer complete, permanent products. Instead, they are fragmented into live services, often centered around time-limited mechanics such as loot boxes, gacha systems, seasonal cosmetics, and battle passes. These systems frequently require real-world money, yet offer no guarantee of permanent access. When a games servers are shut down, consumers lose access not only to the game itself, but to content they paid for, sometimes over many years and hundreds of euros. This is more than a financial issue. It is also a cultural one. When a game disappears, so too does a part of our digital heritage. The shutdown of Halo 2 in 2010 is an example: players famously stayed in its online lobby for weeks, unwilling to let go of a community and world they loved. Today, this story risks repeating itself at scale. Only now, consumers are losing not just access, but money and memories tied to monetized, time-limited content. I would strongly urge the Commission to consider: Legal clarity and consumer protections around what happens to purchased digital content when online services end Consumers should have the right to access a functional version of what they paid for, even if multiplayer or live features are removed. Limitations on predatory monetization, especially where content is gated behind real-money transactions with built-in FOMO mechanics Loot boxes, gacha rolls, and battle passes should be subject to fairness requirements, especially when combined with time limits, unclear odds, or psychological manipulation. Preservation mechanisms or obligations for publishers If a game must go offline, publishers could be encouraged (or required) to release offline or archival versions, or provide users with a local access backup. Gaming is more than entertainment. It is an artistic medium, a social space, and a core part of modern European culture. I hope the Digital Fairness Act will reflect that by protecting both consumer rights and the cultural memory that digital games carry. Sincerely, A Consumer and Gamer from the EU
